segments.s498-g57: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش (his/its), so ‘his army.’ ز is the archaic poetic preposition (از) meaning ‘from,’ and دریا is its object ‘the sea.’ بیکسو is an adverbial phrase ‘to one side/aside,’ and شدند is the past plural of شدن (‘they became/moved’); together بیکسو شدند means ‘they moved/withdrew to one side.’ Note the poetic ordering: possessor+noun, then prepositional phrase, then adverb, then verb. Archaic forms: ز for از and the adverbial بیکسو.
segments.s498-g58: بران here means ‘upon/there’ (lit. ‘on that’), pointing to the place just mentioned. نیستان is the direct object ‘reed-bed.’ آتش اندر زدند is a compact verbal phrase: آتش ‘fire’ + اندر (archaic ‘in/into’) + زدند (past plural of زدن). In this poetic collocation ‘آتش اندر زدند’ means ‘they set (it) on fire’ or ‘they set fire to (the reed-bed).’ Note the archaic preposition اندر and the use of زدند for ‘set/strike.’
segments.s498-g59: بکشتند is the past plural verb ‘they killed’ (note the older prefixed ب- in past forms common in epic diction). چندان here functions as part of a correlative/result construction ‘چندان ... که’ meaning ‘so many/much that.’ ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’ (modern از), and شیران is the plural subject/object ‘lions.’ که introduces the result clause beginning with راه. Word order places the verb before the quantity for poetic effect: ‘they killed so many of the lions that…’.
segments.s498-g60: راه is the subject of the result clause (‘the way/the road’). به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase ‘suddenly/at once.’ تنگ شد is ‘became narrow’ (تنگ + شد, past singular); note that شد is singular to agree with راه which was fronted earlier. بر سپاه uses بر (here ‘for/against’ in an archaic sense) to mark the affected party ‘the army.’ Together: ‘the way suddenly became narrow for the army.’
segments.s498-g9: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; here it introduces the item ('their fish'). 'ماهی بدیشان' literally 'fish for them/theirs' (بدیشان = for/to them), and 'همی' is the older continuous/emphatic particle attached to the participial/adjectival 'خوردنی' (here 'edible' or 'to be eaten'), so the whole chunk means 'the fish that they eat / their edible fish.'
segments.s498-g10: 'به جایی' = 'to/into any place' (جایی = 'any place/somewhere'). 'نبد' is an archaic negative copula/verb meaning 'is not / does not become / is not fitting.' 'راه آوردنی' is a deverbal adjective/gerund from آوردن meaning here 'fit/worthy of bringing or mentioning' (i.e. 'to be brought'). The phrase therefore says it is not suitable to bring/take it anywhere.
segments.s498-g11: 'شگفت' is an exclamatory/nominal word meaning 'a wonder' or 'it was strange'; 'اندر ایشان' = 'among/within them'; 'سکندر بماند' is a simple past/perfect imperfective sense where 'بماند' (here past form) means 'he remained' or 'he was left/stood (in amazement)'. Grammatically this is a verbless/exclamatory front + locative phrase followed by the subject + verb 'remain'.
segments.s498-g12: 'ز دریا' = 'from the sea.' 'همی' again marks continuous/iterative action ('kept/repeatedly'). 'نام یزدان' = 'the name of God' (نام = name, یزدان = God). 'بخواند' here is used in the sense 'to call upon / to cry out (a name)' rather than 'to read'; the whole clause means 'he repeatedly called on the name of God (from the sea)'.
segments.s498-g13: 'هم‌انگاه' is an adverb meaning 'immediately / at that very moment.' 'کوهی برآمد' = 'a mountain rose/appeared' (کوهی here used as a noun 'a mountain' — older Persian freely forms nouns from adjectives). 'ز آب' = 'out of/from the water.' Word order is adverb → subject → verb → source phrase.
segments.s498-g14: 'بدو' = 'to it / unto it' (here an adverbial/dative particle referring back to the mountain). 'پاره شد' is a compound meaning 'was split/became sundered' (پاره = torn/piece; شد = became). 'زرد' = 'yellow' and 'چون آفتاب' = 'like the sun' (چون used as the simile 'like'). Together: 'it was split and turned yellow like the sun.'
segments.s498-g15: Subject + numeral/adjective + noun + verb: 'سکندر' (Alexander) + 'یکی' here = 'a certain / one' (introducing an indefinite boat) + 'تیز کشتی' = 'a swift boat' (تیز = swift) + 'بجست' is the past of بجستن meaning 'he leapt/sprang.' The older past form 'ب-' prefix and short perfect is standard in Ferdowsi.
segments.s498-g16: 'که' introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). 'آن را' is the definite object 'it' plus the object marker را. 'ببیند' in classical/epic usage is the present-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may see / might see' used after که for purpose. 'به دیده درست' = 'with correct/true sight' i.e. 'with his own/clear eyes.' Word order is subordinating particle → object → verb → instrumental phrase.
segments.s498-g33: وزان جایگه (a fused prepositional phrase + noun) = 'from that place.' وزان is a poetic compound meaning 'from that' or 'and from that,' followed by جایگه 'place/spot.' لشکر اندر کشید is a verb phrase with the subject لشکر 'the army' and the past tense verb کشید 'drew/pulled' (here 'moved in/withdrew inward'); اندر 'into/within' marks the inward direction. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the locative phrase precedes the subject and verb.
segments.s498-g34: یکی آبگیری نو آمد پدید is an independent clause: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces the noun آبگیری 'water-collection/sluice/reservoir' qualified by نو 'new.' آمد پدید is a compound verbal expression meaning 'came forth/appeared' (poetic inversion of پدید آمد). The numeral/indefinite precedes the noun as in Classical Persian.
segments.s498-g35: به گرد اندرش نی بسان درخت: به گرد + گرد = 'around/about'; اندرش = اندر + ـش (in/within + enclitic 'its') giving 'around it.' نی 'reed' is the substantive and بسان 'like/as' introduces the simile درخت 'tree.' The phrase thus means 'around it reeds (stood) like trees' with the verb understood or omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s498-g36: تو گفتی که چوب چنارست سخت: تو گفتی 'you said' (past, 2nd person) introduces a reported clause. که 'that' marks the subordinate content clause. چوب 'wood' is the subject, چنارست is a fused predicate (چنار + ـست = 'is plane-tree'), and سخت 'hard' is a predicative adjective. The clause literally reads 'you said that the wood is plane-tree, hard.'
segments.s498-g37: ز پنجه فزون بود بالای اوی: ز 'from' introduces the comparison phrase 'from the hand/span' (پنجه here = span/hand/measure). فزون بود = 'was greater/exceeded' (فزون = more/greater, بود = was). بالای اوی 'above it' places the comparison relative to the thing (اوی = its). The construction is comparative: 'it was above (i.e., greater than) a hand/span.'
segments.s498-g38: چهل رش بپیمود پهنای اوی: چهل 'forty' numerally modifies رش (a unit/measure here), بپیمود is the past form of پیمودن 'to traverse/span/measure,' and پهنای اوی = 'its width/breadth.' The clause says the breadth measured/extended forty (rash), with poetic order (verb follows numeral phrase).
segments.s498-g39: همه خانه‌ها کرده از چوب و نی: همه خانه‌ها 'all the houses' is the topic; کرده is a past participle/verb meaning 'made' or 'had been made' and here functions as the predicate. از چوب و نی 'of wood and reed' indicates the material. The typical Classical Persian order places the topic before the verb and the material after the verb.
segments.s498-g40: زمینش هم از نی فروبرده پی: زمینش = زمین + enclitic ـش 'its ground'; هم 'also/too' links this clause to the previous; از نی 'with reed' indicates the material used; فروبرده is a compound participial/past form meaning 'had been plunged/thrust down' (i.e., driven/packed down), and پی here means 'foundation/foot.' Together: 'its ground too had reed driven down as foundation.'
segments.s498-g25: فروبرد is a past transitive verb (felt here as 'sank' or 'drove down'); in this epic-line the verb appears before its object (verb-first word order). کشتی is the object 'ship'. هم اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase ('also/in haste' → 'quickly'), where هم links this action to what follows and اندر شتاب ('in haste') modifies the verb.
segments.s498-g26: هم here links the second clause ('also' / 'at the same time'); آن کوه is the noun phrase 'that mountain'. شد + ناپدید is a light verb + predicative adjective construction meaning 'became vanished' → 'disappeared'. اندر آب ('in the water') is a locative phrase marking where it vanished.
segments.s498-g27: سپاه سکندر is a possessive noun phrase 'the army/host of Alexander'. همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle marking an ongoing state ('was still/kept on'). خیره ماند is a compound: خیره ('staring/astonished') + ماند ('remained') = 'stood astonished/stunned.'
segments.s498-g28: همی again marks continued action. هرکسی (here meaning 'every person') is the subject of the verb. نام یزدان is the object phrase 'the name of God' and بخواند is the narrative-form verb 'he/they would call/recite' (here: 'kept calling/invoking').
segments.s498-g29: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here placed before the speaker رومی: poetic word order 'to him said the Roman'). که introduces the reported clause. دانش بهست is an old/poetic copular clause meaning 'knowledge is sufficient' (بهست = 'it suffices').
segments.s498-g30: که again introduces the content of the speech. داننده is 'the knower' (one who knows). بر هر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon every person'. The final بر مهست is a comparative/likening: مهست (poetic) 'is the moon' or 'like the moon' — together saying the knower stands above everyone like the moon above (people). The clause uses classical poetic word order and a simile.
segments.s498-g31: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). شاه is a vocative or topical address 'O king'. رفتی and گشتی are past/second-person forms used in classical Persian to state the protasis ('if you go and you become ...'); تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed.'
segments.s498-g32: پر از خون is a prepositional adjectival phrase 'full of blood'. شدی is the verb 'became/was made'. جان چندین سپاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the life(s) of several hosts/armies' (جان governing چندین سپاه), so the clause says those lives 'became full of blood' — a poetic result clause of the conditional.
segments.s498-g49: وزان is an archaic compound (و + زان) meaning 'and from that' and introduces the source phrase. بیشه (preposed) marks the location 'that grove/thicket', followed by the noun گزدم (scorpion) as the subject. چو + simile (چو آتش به رنگ) compares the scorpion's appearance to fire; به here introduces the manner/quality ('in the color of'), linking رنگ ('color') to آتش ('fire').
segments.s498-g50: جهان شد uses شد (past of شدن) as a copular/impersonal verb 'became' — the result clause that follows. بران is an adverbial 'upon/thereon' (= 'for those'), and خفتگان is the plural participial noun 'the sleeping' (those who sleep). تار و تنگ is a coordinated predicate (two adjectives), where تار is a poetic/archaic adjective meaning 'dark' (not listed in the provided senses) and تنگ is 'narrow/close'; together they describe the state brought upon the sleepers.
segments.s498-g51: به هر گوشه‌ای places the action locatively ('in/at every corner'); در فراوان functions as an adverbial phrase 'in abundance' (فراوان modifying the verb). بمرد is an older poetic verbal form meaning 'died' (here functioning as a past action); word order puts the locative/adverbials before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s498-g52: This nominal group lists the victims: بزرگان دانا is a noun + adjective ('great ones, wise'), then و connects to مردان گرد. مردان is plural 'men' (here epic 'heroes'), and گرد follows as an attributive element indicating 'around/gathered' (a descriptive/postpositive sense); overall this is a coordinated noun phrase functioning as the subject/object of بمرد.
segments.s498-g53: ز یک‌سو is a simple locative phrase 'from one side'; فراوان here is an adverb 'in great numbers/plentifully' and precedes the verb بیامد 'came' (past), which takes the animates گراز ('boars') as its subject. The preposed adverbial order emphasizes the sudden, numerous arrival.
segments.s498-g54: چو introduces a simile 'like': الماس is the comparison term 'diamond', and دندانهای دراز is the noun phrase 'their long teeth' (دندانهای being the plural possessive/construct form). The adjective دراز follows the noun in the typical Persian order, qualifying the teeth.
segments.s498-g55: ز دست دگر is a compound locative/explanatory phrase 'from another hand/quarter' (دست here can mean 'side' or 'power'); شیر مهتر is a noun ('lion') followed by مهتر 'chief/greater', so 'a lion, the chief/greater one'. The second ز before گاو functions poetically as a comparative 'than/from' introducing گاو ('ox/cow'), so the phrase compares or relates the lion to cattle.
segments.s498-g56: که introduces a clause of explanation/reason ('for/that'), باِ جنگ ایشان 'with their fighting' places جنگ ('battle/fight') as the object of با and ایشان as its possessor/agent. نبد is a negated past/existential verb 'was not' or 'there was not', so the predicate 'زور و تاو' ('strength and radiance') follows as what did not exist — i.e., no strength or radiance opposed them. The coordinating و links the two nouns forming the compound predicate.
segments.s498-g1: همی رفت is a classical progressive/perfective formula meaning 'kept going' (همی marking continuous or repeated action + رفت past of رفتن). منزل به منزل is a reduplicated adverbial phrase 'from stage to stage / place to place', and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'along/on the road' that frames the motion.
segments.s498-g2: ز here has a causal/ablative force 'because of / from'; رنجه is used as a noun 'hardship/toil' (not a verb); مانده is the past participle/verb 'remained/stood' and is predicated of یکسر سپاه (inversion is poetic: predicate precedes the subject). یکسر qualifies سپاه as 'the entire/whole army.'
segments.s498-g3: ز شهر برهمن is an ablative phrase 'from the city of the Brahmin'; به جایی رسید is a simple past clause 'arrived at a place' (رسید third-person past). The order places the origin before the verb, standard in classical narrative.
segments.s498-g4: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a' used poetically; بی‌کران (boundless) and ژرف (deep) are prepositive adjectives qualifying دریا (sea). بدید is the archaic past form (ب + دید) meaning 'saw' (classical past where modern Persian would use دید).
segments.s498-g5: بسان زنان is a simile 'like women' placed before the noun it qualifies. مرد پوشیده روی is a noun phrase 'men with covered/veiled faces' where پوشیده is a past-participle/adjective 'covered' modifying روی 'face' (i.e. 'with face covered'). Word order (qualifier before noun phrase) is typical in poetry.
segments.s498-g6: همی رفت again marks continuous past 'kept going'. با جامه و رنگ و بوی is a comitative/instrumental phrase 'with garments and (a) appearance/color and scent' describing how they went — i.e. they were dressed and perfumed. The conjunctions simply link the listed items.
segments.s498-g7: زبانها is the subject 'languages/tongues'. The construction نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'; تازی refers to Arabic/Arab, and خسروی here functions as 'royal/khusrawi' (i.e. courtly Persian) as an item in the list of tongues.
segments.s498-g8: The negation continues: نه ترکی نه چینی و نه پهلوی = 'neither Turkish nor Chinese nor Pahlavi.' Each item is another language name; پهلوی here denotes the Pahlavi (Middle Persian) tongue rather than the literal sense 'side/flank.'
segments.s498-g41: نشایست is an archaic/poetic impersonal form (compare نشاید) meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'it did not suit'; here it carries a negative sense. بد is an adverb 'badly/ill' intensifying the judgment. در نیستان is a prepositional phrase 'in the reed-bed' (در = in), and بسی ('much' / 'very') intensifies the whole clause. Together this segment says roughly 'it was very unsuitable (in) the reed-bed.'
segments.s498-g42: ز here means 'because of' (causal use). شوری = 'saltiness'. نخورد is past tense with negative sense ('did not drink/consume'); with the universal/indefinite هرکسی following, the negative verb yields the sense 'no one/anyone did X'. آب او = 'its water' (او = third‑person pronoun referring back). So the clause means 'because of the saltiness, no one drank its water.'
segments.s498-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بگذشت is a 3rd‑person past 'passed' (the subject is implicit). زان آب = 'from/after that water' (زان = 'from that'); جایی رسید = 'reached a place' (جایی = 'somewhere/a place', رسید = 'arrived/reached'). The whole segment: 'When it/they passed that water, (they) reached a place.'
segments.s498-g44: که introduces the relative/subordinate clause 'that/where'. آمد is past 'came'. یکی is the indefinite article 'a certain' or simply 'a'. ژرف modifies دریا (ژرف دریا = 'deep sea'). پدید (here postposed) is the verb 'appeared/was revealed'. Thus: '...where a deep sea appeared.'
segments.s498-g45: جهان is the subject 'the world', خرم is a predicate adjective 'joyful/lush'. و links the next clause. آب begins a new noun phrase, and چون is the simile 'like/as'. انگبین (here 'nectar') compares the water to sweet, delightful nectar. So: 'The world was joyous, and the water like nectar.'
segments.s498-g46: همی is a durative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/still'. مشک is the noun 'musk'. بویید here is used in the sense 'gave off a scent / was perfumed' (the verb can mean emit scent). روی زمین = 'upon the earth / over the land' (روی = on/upon, زمین = earth/ground). The clause: 'the earth kept/perpetually smelled of musk.'
segments.s498-g47: بخوردند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they ate' (context makes this the eating of the people). و is the conjunction 'and'. کردند is past 'they made/did'. آهنگ خواب is a noun phrase 'intention/plan for sleep' (آهنگ = intention/aim; خواب = sleep). Together: 'They ate and prepared/intended to sleep.'
segments.s498-g48: بسی functions here as an intensifier meaning 'many' or 'a great number'. مار = 'snake' and پیچان is an adjective/participle 'twisting / winding' (i.e., 'twisting snakes'). برآمد is past 'rose/appeared/emerged'. ز آب = 'from the water' (ز = from, آب = water). So: 'Many twisting snakes rose up from the water.'
segments.s498-g17: This clause is an introductory main clause: 'یکی' is an indefinite subject meaning 'one/someone' (here 'one of them'); 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (classical narrative past). 'زان فیلسوفان' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of those philosophers' that identifies which 'one' is speaking, and 'به شاه' is the indirect object 'to the king' (preposition + indirect object follows the verb in this older/poetic order).
segments.s498-g18: 'که' introduces the reported content clause ('that ...'); word order is somewhat inverted: 'بر ژرف دریا' = 'across/on the deep sea' (prepositional phrase), 'ترا را' is the 2nd-person enclitic pronoun + object marker (literally 'for you' / 'to you' with emphatic/definite marking), and 'نیست راه' expresses the predicate 'there is no way' (نیست = 'is not', راه = 'way'). The verb 'نیست' is present simple and negates existence of a path.
segments.s498-g19: 'بمان' is the imperative 'remain / wait'. 'تا' introduces the subordinate clause of time/purpose and is followed by the subjunctive/present-jussive 'ببیند' ('may see' / 'sees' in the sense 'until someone sees'). 'مر او را کسی' places the objects before the postposed subject 'کسی' (a common classical word order): 'مر' is an archaic contracted object form (mara/me), 'او را' = 'him' (with object marker), so the sense is 'until someone sees him (for me / on my behalf)'. The real subject 'کسی' appears at the end.
segments.s498-g20: This is a relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who / that'). 'بهره ندارد' is a verb phrase: 'بهره' = 'share/portion' and 'ندارد' = 'does not have' (present). 'ز دانش بسی' is a prepositional phrase 'of (much) learning/knowledge' where 'ز' = 'of/from', 'دانش' = 'knowledge', and 'بسی' functions as a quantifier meaning 'much / abundant' — together: 'who does not have much share of knowledge' (= 'who has little learning').
segments.s498-g21: This is a coordinated prepositional phrase listing sources: 'ز رومی' = 'from the Romans' and 'از مردم پارسی' = 'and from the Persian people'. The two prepositions 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') and 'از' are used in parallel to introduce who embarked.
segments.s498-g22: 'بدان' is the contracted compound 'به آن' = 'into/onto that'; 'کشتی' = 'ship' is the goal; 'اندر نشستند' is the past plural verb 'they sat down / they embarked' (classical phrasing for 'boarded'). The numeral 'سی' (thirty) in the original line specifies how many, but the verb phrase is ordered so that the motion into the ship precedes the explicit subject/number in poetic word order. Note: some lexical items are archaic/contractions here.
segments.s498-g23: This opening uses 'یکی' = 'a certain / one' to introduce an entity. 'زرد ماهی' = 'a yellow fish'. The word 'بد' here is an archaic literary form of the past copula 'بُد' (was), so the phrase reads 'There was a yellow fish.' 'آن لخت کوه' is an appositive noun phrase 'that naked/bare mountain' describing the fish (i.e. appearing as a bare rock/mountain).
segments.s498-g24: 'هم‌انگه' (همان‌گاه) is a temporal adverb 'at that moment'; 'چو' introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'; 'تنگ' here denotes 'the narrows / strait' (a narrow passage), 'اندر آمد' is the past verb 'entered', and 'گروه' (group/band) is the noun that will be acted upon or that acts — the subject/object ordering is flexible in classical diction, so 'when it entered the narrows, the band ...'.

sentences.s498-s29: His army withdrew from the sea to one side; there they set the reed-bed on fire.
sentences.s498-s30: They killed so many of the lions that the road suddenly became narrow for the army.
sentences.s498-s5: Their fish may be edible for them, but it is not fit to be carried to any place.
sentences.s498-s6: Alexander remained astonished among them; from the sea he kept calling on the name of God.
sentences.s498-s7: At that instant a mountain rose out of the water; it split into pieces and became yellow like the sun.
sentences.s498-s8: Alexander sprang into a swift boat so that he could see it with his own eyes.
sentences.s498-s17: From that place the army drew in, and a new water-collection (reservoir/sluice) came into being.
sentences.s498-s18: Around it reeds stood like trees; you said that the wood of the plane-tree is hard.
sentences.s498-s19: Its height exceeded the reach of the hand, and its breadth spanned forty (units of) rash.
sentences.s498-s20: All the houses were made of wood and reed, and its ground, too, had reed driven down as foundation.
sentences.s498-s13: The ship plunged quickly into the water, and that mountain too disappeared beneath the waves.
sentences.s498-s14: Alexander's host stood stunned, and every man kept crying the name of God.
sentences.s498-s15: The Roman said to him, 'Knowledge is enough; for the knower stands above every person like the moon.'
sentences.s498-s16: If, O king, you go and are ruined, the lives of many hosts will be filled with blood.
sentences.s498-s25: From that thicket a scorpion, like fire in color; the world became for those sleepers dark and narrow.
sentences.s498-s26: In every corner, in great numbers, there died the great and wise and the gathered men.
sentences.s498-s27: From one side swarmed many boars; their long teeth were like diamonds.
sentences.s498-s28: From another quarter came a lion, chief among the cattle; for in their fighting there was no strength or radiance (to match it).
sentences.s498-s1: They kept going from stage to stage along the road; because of the road's hardship the whole army remained (behind).
sentences.s498-s2: From the Brahmin's city he reached a place and saw a boundless, deep sea.
sentences.s498-s3: Like women, the men with veiled faces kept going, adorned with garments, color, and scent.
sentences.s498-s4: Their tongues/languages were neither Arabic nor 'khusrawi' (royal/Persian), neither Turkish, Chinese, nor Pahlavi.
sentences.s498-s21: It was not fitting in the reed-bed for long; because of its saltiness no one drank its water.
sentences.s498-s22: When they passed that water, they reached a place where a deep sea appeared.
sentences.s498-s23: The world was joyful, and the water was like nectar; the earth continually smelled of musk.
sentences.s498-s24: They ate and made ready for sleep; many twisting snakes rose up from the water.
sentences.s498-s9: One of those philosophers said to the king that there is no way for you across the deep sea.
sentences.s498-s10: Wait until someone who has little share of learning sees him.
